provenance.source_corpus: Seven year remediation study at the Carrie Bay Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar) farm in the Broughton Archipelago, British Columbia, Canada

source_spans.text: It was fallowed in 1998 after producing 2587 mt of Atlantic salmon in the preceding production cycle.

Chemical remediation, including reductions in total volatile solids (TVS), free sulfides, redox potential and zinc were nearly complete at distances >80 m in 2002 and rapid and significant additional chemical remediation was observed throughout the area in 2003.
Biological remediation was nearing completion at distances z80 m from the netpens, but was not complete under and at shorter distances in 2002 following 4 years of fallow.

Chemical remediation, including reductions in total volatile solids (TVS), free sulfides, redox potential and zinc were nearly complete at distances >80 m in 2002 and rapid and significant additional chemical remediation was observed throughout the area in 2003.

Chemical remediation, including reductions in total volatile solids (TVS), free sulfides, redox potential and zinc were nearly complete at distances >80 m in 2002 and rapid and significant additional chemical remediation was observed throughout the area in 2003.
Sediment concentrations of zinc have declined throughout this study at all distances and were approaching background concentrations in 2002 and 2003.

Chemical remediation, including reductions in total volatile solids (TVS), free sulfides, redox potential and zinc were nearly complete at distances >80 m in 2002 and rapid and significant additional chemical remediation was observed throughout the area in 2003.
The abundance of macrofauna increased to reference values at stations located greater than 95 to 115 m from the netpen’s perimeter in 2001 and 2002, but remained depressed at closer stations.
Fig. 15 indicated that the number of taxa in Carrie Bay sediments was reduced by half at 447 AMS =.

Thus it appears that locating salmon farms over areas with depositional bottoms containing >70% to 80% fines may create adverse benthic effects that extend to at least 200 m from the netpens and that may take 5 to 6 years for chemical remediation and somewhat longer for complete biological remediation.

Thus it appears that locating salmon farms over areas with depositional bottoms containing >70% to 80% fines may create adverse benthic effects that extend to at least 200 m from the netpens and that may take 5 to 6 years for chemical remediation and somewhat longer for complete biological remediation.
